storm.summary_layperson: Light rain has already begun at the cove, with the main storm complex 7 miles away — heavier rain and lightning are likely by 10 PM, and a Flood Watch is in effect through 6 AM.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Long Island Sound water (69°F) is warmer than the air (64°F) tonight, so no marine cooling layer is in place to deflect or weaken incoming storms.
storm.detail_html:
A complex of showers and thunderstorms extends across Westchester and Fairfield County, with KOKX showing a peak return of 44 dBZ and the nearest identified cell at 6.3 miles NW (35 dBZ) tracking eastward toward the cove. CAPE is nearly absent at 10 J/kg — the atmosphere has no meaningful buoyancy for severe dynamics. This is primarily a heavy rain and lightning event, not a severe thunderstorm scenario. The active Flood Watch through 6 AM is calibrated to 1–2 inches of rainfall over the period, consistent with the high-shear/low-CAPE setup producing sustained stratiform and embedded convective rain rather than brief, explosive cells. Light rain is already reaching the cove (RYC rain rate 0.05 in/hr; 1–3 mm recorded in the past hour at Port Chester and Rye APRS stations).

The typical Riverside coastal buffer is not in play tonight. The "Sound effect" — where cool water creates a stable marine layer that undercuts incoming convection — requires warm air over cool water. Tonight the relationship is inverted: Long Island Sound water is 69°F against an inland air temperature of only 64°F, a −4.5°F delta. No stable cooling layer exists over the water. Storms crossing into the cove area will not be weakened by the Sound transit; conditions here should mirror surrounding inland communities in Greenwich, Stamford, and Rye.

Storm motion was not radar-tracked — no usable prior KOKX scan was available in the 15–35 minute look-back window. The arrival engine is using HRRR 700mb steering: ~6.6 kt toward 92° (due east), giving a closing-speed component of 3.1 kt toward the cove from the NW. That slow approach is why the arrival window is so wide. A second cell cluster (37 dBZ) sits 17–18 miles to the west and is closing faster at ~6 kt, tracking roughly 15–20 minutes behind the lead cell.

The ETA engine places first impact around 9:52 PM, with the credible window spanning 9:06–10:38 PM. Because there is no marine-layer mitigating adjustment and no punch-through amplification, the window is symmetric — no reason to lean early or late. Upstream APRS stations in Port Chester (N1TKS, 4 mi WSW) and Rye Brook (GW4034, 5 mi WSW) are useful sentinels: a gust spike at either station typically leads cove arrival by 20–35 minutes at this closing speed.

What would change this call upward: any approaching cell intensifying above 45 dBZ within 25 miles, or an upstream APRS station spiking above 35 kt before the main reflectivity arrives. With 10 J/kg CAPE, both are unlikely — but 38 kt of low-level shear provides some organizational support for isolated stronger cells. Clearing is likely after 11 PM–1 AM as the system exits eastward.

arrival.blurb: Nearest cell 6 mi NW closing at 3 kt on model-estimated motion; wide window reflects the slow approach — no local marine-layer adjustment in effect.

wind.tactics_blurb: Not a racing window — storms are arriving around 9–10 PM with the cove exposed in ENE gradient flow. If forced onto the water before 9 PM, slight advantage on the left/west side as ENE flow wraps around Greenwich Point.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Tonight's wind is entirely gradient-driven — Regime B-moderate. The sea-breeze two-ingredient test fails completely: inland air (64°F) is actually cooler than Long Island Sound water (69°F), giving an inverted delta of −4.5°F where a positive 5°F is required. The ensemble shows coastal_pct at just 5% with zero inland southerly flow. No thermal driver exists, and no sea-breeze maturation direction correction applies. The synoptic ENE gradient is what you get.

Authoritative mean wind speed is 8.5 kt (ensemble), with KBDR at 15 kt gusting 22 on the open coast and RYC averaging 8.6 kt in the cove. The near-2× siting gap reflects significant shelter from Greenwich Point in ENE flow — the cove's N–S axis is largely perpendicular to the incoming ENE direction, and the eastern wall blocks much of that component. Expect real-water speeds in the 8–13 kt range while KBDR reads 14–16 kt. HRRR 23Z f01–f03 (20:00–22:00 EDT) shows 13–15 kt ENE, consistent with the observed gradient. Forecast hours 17:00–19:00 EDT predate the 23Z cycle and are estimated from the NWS gradient forecast (NE 12–16 mph) and current observations.

Direction is backing persistently from ENE toward NE and NNE overnight. HRRR shows 60° at 20:00 EDT, 51° by 23:00, 30° by 01:00 — a system-scale rotation with no oscillations to play tactically. From 21:00 EDT onward, storm outflow gust fronts could produce abrupt direction spikes in unpredictable directions; the gradient direction ceases to be the operative wind field once a gust front arrives.

In ENE flow, Greenwich Point shelters the eastern side of the cove. The best available pressure sits on the left/west side (Field Point / Belle Haven) where flow curves around the southern tip of the Point. With storms arriving by 9–10 PM, any meaningful racing window closed around 20:30 EDT as the lead cell entered 5-mile range. Tonight is not a racing night.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 offline — UConn ERDDAP returned 404; over-water wind and sea-state data unavailable
Storm arrival ETA uses HRRR model-estimated motion (no radar-tracked speed); confidence low — lean on the 9:06–10:38 PM window rather than the 9:52 PM point estimate
HRRR hourly forecast unavailable for 17:00–19:00 EDT (pre-dating the 23Z model cycle); those hours estimated from NWS gradient forecast and current observations
